titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Thomas Heath to Sir Robert Chalmers, 10 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Thomas Heath

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Thomas Little Heath (1861-1940) to Robert Chalmers (1858 - 1938) concerning the salary of Resident Magistrates who are promoted from the second to the first class while on active service.Sir Thomas Little Heath was a British civil servant. From 1913 to 1919 he occupied the position of permanent secretary to the Treasury. Sir Robert Chalmers was sent to Ireland as replacement under-secretary for Sir Matthew Nathan, who had resigned along with Augustine Birrell, his chief secretary, following the outbreak of the Easter Rising in 1916.

correspAction.placeName: Treasury Chambers, Whitehall, London SW, England

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Any reply to this letter should be addressed to-
THE SECRETARY,
TREASURY,
WHITEHALL, LONDON, S.W.,
and the following number quoted. 16723 16 10 July 1916 Sir, The Lord Commissioners of His Majesty's
Treasury have received from the Treasury Remembrancer copies
of communications with your Department on the subject of
the position of Resident Magistrates who are promoted
from the second to the first class while on active service,
as regards the balance of civil salary to be paid to them. I am to request that you will inform the Lords
Justices that My Lords note that the Attorney General is
of opinion that in such cases the promoted officer cannot
legally be deprived of the civil pay attaching to his new
class, abated by his military pay and allowances. I am to observe that the effect of this decision would be
almost the same in substance as if the balance of civil
pay were not increased, since under the Attorney-General's
ruling military allowances as well as military pay would be
deducted, whereas under the Treasury Circulars military pay
alone is deducted. The allowances would in most cases
exceed the difference between the pay of the first and
second class. My Lords are nevertheless willing to accept the
solution which is proposed, namely that the Treasury
Circular should be applied in the case of any similar
promotions The Under Secretary,
Dublin Castle.
promotions proposed to be made hereafter but should
not apply to promotions already made. I am,
Sir,
Your obedient Servant, J.L. Heath The signature is handwritten not typed.
